correct: While working for IBM, I have discovered it is a multi-cultural environment: I have worked with a(?) Japanese team, Australian team, Hong Kong team & a European team. (If it is more than one team, you would say "teams" for each nationality that you have worked for more than one of. But if it is only one for each, you just say "a" before the first and last one as I did. If some of those nationalities you did work with multiple teams and not others, than you would put "a" before each one that you only worked with one of and use "teams" for each one that you have worked with more than one of. Also, do you mean to say "branches" instead of "team"? Usually when describing the department(s) of a company that is disconnected from its official headquarters, it is called a "branch". But if you just mean that you have worked with individual people from these different nationalities, "team(s)" is more appropriate.)

You can watch things when you observe them, but to "see" something that is moving does not focus on what the something is doing, rather on the something. Think - if I use a noun, I usually need an article (a, an, the). But which article? Ok, it's a little difficult but: - use it for nouns about unique things. For example the sun (the sun is unique, there's only one sun so there's no need to say a sun because there aren't any others). A + AN - for general things which we don't want to be specific about. For example a book, table, apple... The first time we say the noun we usually just use 'a' because it's new, but the second, third, fourth...time we say 'the' because now we are talking about something specific. For example: man is walking with dog, dog is big' man (any man it doesn't matter who at this moment). dog (any dog, it doesn't matter what kind of dog yet). dog (now we are focussing on a particular dog and we say 'the' because we don't want people to confuse this dog with any dog or dogs in general). first time ('the', because it's not any time - it's the first time) foreiner, article, blog , ('a', because it could be any foreigner, any blog -it doesn't matter at this point). author ('the', because only one person wrote the blog so it's not anybody - it's one specific person).Sometimes we say nouns articles. For example when saying instructions or orders 'Marek, go to bed!!' (not the bed or a bed).

text: But the temperature was over 30 degree today.
correct: But the temperature was over 30 (degrees) today. [In spoken English, you can start a sentence with "but." In more formal written English, it is better to use "however": "However, the temperature was over 30 degrees today." The "proper" way to use "but" is to join the two sentences: "I feel that autumn will come soon, but the temperature today was over 30 degrees." This comment really applies to "so" in the previous sentence as well. Words like and, so, but, and because are called conjunctions, and their "proper" use is to join two sentences together.]

text: (D) I think this TV set has some trouble.
correct: (D) I think this TV set has some problem. [I would only describe a conscious agent as "having trouble (with something)." I would say "I am having trouble with this TV set," but not "This TV set is having trouble," unless I was intentionally anthropomorphizing it.]

text: (B) I have been healthy since I came here last year, so I have never seen a doctor.
correct: (B) I have been healthy since I came here last year, so I have not seen a doctor. (Because the "since" clause modifies the verb in the first independent clause, "I have been healthy", the "never" in the second independent clause would be unqualified, implying that you've *never* seen a doctor -- ever. You could add a "since then" to qualify it, but it's easier to just say "not" instead of "never".)

correct: (A-2) I had been looking forward to traveling abroad, but since I saw groups of Japanese tourists wherever I went, I got fed up with it. [This is also possible, but less natural than (A-1), because the past reference time would normally be a specific time at which the speaker stopped looking forward to traveling abroad, since "had been looking" implies continuous action up to that past reference time.]

text: (A) I haven't found my umbrella which I lost a week ago.
correct: (A) I haven't found the umbrella that/which I lost a week ago. [The phrase "that I lost a week ago" (or "which I lost a week ago") is called a restrictive relative clause. It is restrictive because it restricts the range of objects which the phrase could refer to-- not any umbrella, but the one with the further property that I lost it a week ago. Some grammarians insist that one must use "that" with a restrictive clause. Others say that either "that" or "which" is acceptable. Both are used in practice. It is unnatural to use "my" in this sentence because most people only have one umbrella. Therefore "my umbrella" already specifies a single object, and it is unnatural to add a restrictive clause to that description.]

correct: Sudan is a country of different races, languages, and religions, but specific parts in the north are more educated than other parts of Sudan. This is because during British rule the south and west of Sudan were closed areas and the government didn't allow any cultural developmental. So, soon after liberation the Sudanese found themselves with big challenge of how to rule this wide country where the north Sudanese were more educated. The government was ruled by them and other citizens felt like this government doesn't represent them. The biggest historical mistake is that this government did not change British policy of closed lands. As result of this huge mistake, the racism grew between Sudanese populations and rapidly the southern Sudanese took up arms to get their rights in Sudan. At that point, no Sudanese, including southern Sudanese, had the idea for a separation. They just wanted their rights in their country as whole Sudan. And, as days go by, and wars burned houses and killed children and women, the idea of separation from Sudan arose. In 2005, the happiest year of Sudanese history, the government and (splm) stopped the war in South Sudan the Nifasha Peace Agreement has born. The government promised a lot political changes: the system became democratic and a successful election also occurred. Southern Sudanese can rule their own lands by the new federal system. In the Nifasha Peace Agreement, the government sponsored the referendum right after six years of the treaty. This period was supposed to be the rehabilitation period of the wars affect on the south. The south took more than 50 percent of the oil to rehabilitate southern Sudan by SPLA. Due to the bad situation in the south and huge corruption, not many changes took place in health and education and most of the money was spent on south Sudanese army. Due to the environment which lacks any trust, now the 6 years is running out fast and the Sudanese face a referendum one month from now. The news is not good about the south, because a lot of politicians see the separation as the start of a new phase of war in Sudan because most of the oil in Sudan lies in the boundaries between south and north. And these boundaries have not been defined yet, so the Sudanese are worried about witnessing another endless war. The situation is very tense and everybody is expecting the worst, but there is hope that the referendum will lead to unity. And if that occurs, it will be the true liberation of Sudan and a promising future. We pray for our children to be raised in a united Sudan without bloodshed.

Comment: In English, we usually "learn about" things rather than "get to know" things. The only exception I can think of at the moment is when the object is a person. Then either works, but they mean slightly different things. When you "get to know" a person, then you are personally familiar with them. When you "learn about" a person, then you have gained information describing the person. While in principle I suppose the same could apply to a place (such as Hawaii), I have never heard it in practice.

text: In Japan, it's common that a waitress brings everything-dishes, chop sticks, appetizers- to her customer's table at one time.

Here on Lang-8 we don't (yet) have the luxury of what are called "en dashes" or "em dashes" (indicating the width of our letters N and M). So, double hyphens, IMHO, with spaces on either side indicating dashes, which are something like parentheses, are the clearest way to go. Example: "I was talking to my wife the other night -- baseball season being over -- and she said .............." It's kind of like what we call an "aside," sort of like when an actor turns toward the audience with a little quip: -- baseball season being over --. ;-) ]
